claim.hypotheses_location: Section 4. The current research: “Based on the assumption that mortality salience compromises the availability of cognitive resources, we predict that individuals under mortality salience will be less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts …”
claim.statement: Individuals reminded of their own mortality are less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts than individuals in a control condition.
claim.statement_location: Abstract; Discussion section first paragraph: “We predicted and experimentally demonstrated that individuals under mortality salience would be less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts.”

data.source: Laboratory experiments with student participants recruited on the University of Toulouse campus
data.sample_size: 85 in Experiment 1; 110 usable cases of 115 in Experiment 2 after outlier removal
data.notes: Participants randomly assigned to mortality-salience (Death) vs control (Pain) conditions; additional cognitive-load manipulation in Experiment 2.

method.description: Two between-subjects laboratory experiments manipulated mortality salience (Death vs Pain writing task). Participants then judged moral dilemma scenarios where utilitarian and deontic responses either conflicted (conﬂict problems) or aligned (control problems). Experiment 2 added three levels of concurrent dot-matrix memory load (low, high, very high) presented before each dilemma.
steps: Recruit university students and obtain consent.
Randomly assign participants to mortality-salience condition (Death vs Pain).
Have participants complete the writing task corresponding to their condition.
Present four moral scenarios (two conflict, two control) in counter-balanced order; collect acceptability judgments of the utilitarian action.
Experiment 2 only: before each scenario, show a dot-matrix to memorize (low, high, or very high load); after judgment, participants reproduce the matrix.
Record responses and calculate proportion of utilitarian judgments.
Analyze data using ANOVA and t-tests to test effects of mortality salience and cognitive load.
method.models: ANOVA (mixed; factors: problem type, mortality-salience condition, cognitive load) and planned t-tests
method.outcome_variable: Percentage/frequency of utilitarian responses to moral dilemmas
method.independent_variables: Mortality salience condition (Death vs Pain); Cognitive load level (low, high, very high in Experiment 2); Problem type (conflict vs control)
method.control_variables: Scenario order (counter-balanced); gender and age not used as covariates

results.summary: Across two experiments, participants under mortality salience were significantly less likely to endorse utilitarian solutions in moral conflict dilemmas. In Experiment 1 the effect was specific to conflict problems. In Experiment 2, mortality salience reduced utilitarian responses by roughly the same magnitude (~20 percentage points) as a very high concurrent cognitive load, whereas lower loads did not significantly affect judgments.

numerical_results.unit: percentage of participants endorsing utilitarian action

numerical_results.outcome_name: Utilitarian responses to conflict dilemmas under very high cognitive load (Experiment 2, Pain condition)
Main effect of mortality salience on conflict dilemmas (Experiment 2)

metadata.original_paper_title: Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
